data.title: Is It Too Late To Consider MongoDB (MDB) After Its Recent Share Price Swings?
data.description:
• Investors may be wondering if MongoDB at around US$264 per share still offers value, or if most of the easy gains are already behind it.
  • The stock has returned 1.1% over the last 7 days and 12.3% over the last month, while the year-to-date return sits at a 33.8% decline and the 1-year return is 51.3%.
  • Recent headlines have focused on MongoDB as a key player in cloud databases and developer tooling, with attention on how its products are being used across modern applications. This context helps explain why sentiment has been shifting, even as the share price has seen both sharp pullbacks and strong rebounds.
  • Simply Wall St currently gives MongoDB a valuation score of 1 out of 6. The rest of this article will unpack what that means by comparing several valuation approaches and then finishing with a more holistic way to think about what the stock might be worth.

MongoDB scores just 1/6 on our valuation checks. See what other red flags we found in the full valuation breakdown.

Approach 1: MongoDB Discounted Cash Flow (DCF) Analysis

A Discounted Cash Flow model takes estimates of a company’s future cash flows, then discounts them back to today using a required rate of return. The goal is to arrive at an estimate of what the business might be worth per share based purely on those projected cash flows.

For MongoDB, the model used is a 2 Stage Free Cash Flow to Equity approach, working entirely in US$. The latest twelve month free cash flow sits at about $488.9 million. Analysts provide explicit free cash flow estimates out to 2031, where projections show free cash flow of $1.32b. Simply Wall St then extrapolates beyond the initial analyst window to complete a 10 year path of cash flows.

Discounting those projected cash flows back to today produces an estimated intrinsic value of about $275.78 per share. Against a current share price of around $264, the DCF output points to roughly a 4.1% discount, which is treated here as essentially in line rather than a clear bargain or a clear premium.

Result: ABOUT RIGHT

MongoDB is fairly valued according to our Discounted Cash Flow (DCF), but this can change at a moment's notice. Track the value in your watchlist or portfolio and be alerted on when to act.

Head to the Valuation section of our Company Report for more details on how we arrive at this Fair Value for MongoDB.

Approach 2: MongoDB Price vs Sales

For a business where earnings are not yet a clean guide, revenue can be a more practical yardstick. This makes the P/S ratio a useful way to think about what you are paying for each dollar of MongoDB’s sales.

In general, higher expected growth and lower perceived risk can support a higher “normal” valuation multiple. Slower growth or higher uncertainty tend to pull that multiple down. MongoDB currently trades on a P/S of 8.62x. That sits above the wider IT industry average of 1.80x and also above the peer group average of 6.68x, so on simple comparisons the stock looks expensive relative to both its sector and similar companies.

Simply Wall St’s Fair Ratio framework tries to refine this picture. It estimates what a reasonable P/S could be for MongoDB, at 7.75x, after accounting for factors such as earnings growth, profit margins, risk profile, market cap and industry. Because this Fair Ratio is tailored to the company’s own characteristics, it can be more informative than broad peer or industry averages alone. Comparing 8.62x to the 7.75x Fair Ratio suggests the shares are pricing in a higher valuation than this model implies.

Result: OVERVALUED

data.title: Scorpio Tankers Reshapes Fleet And Earnings Mix With Vessel Sales And Orders
data.description:
• Scorpio Tankers (NYSE:STNG) has agreed to sell six vessels for roughly $300 million as part of a wider fleet reshuffle.
  • The company is pairing these disposals with newbuilding orders for both product and crude tankers, indicating a renewed fleet profile.
  • The combined actions represent a significant repositioning of Scorpio Tankers’ asset base and earnings mix.

For investors tracking NYSE:STNG, this fleet repositioning comes with the stock at $79.74 and returns of 60.5% year to date and 125.0% over the past year. Over the last 5 years, the share price performance of 360.1% places Scorpio Tankers among the more aggressively rerated shipping names in the market.

Active fleet renewal through vessel sales and newbuild commitments can reshape Scorpio Tankers’ cost base, age profile and exposure across product and crude trades. Investors who follow shipping cycles often watch these capital allocation decisions closely, as they can affect earnings sensitivity and how the company competes against peers.

Stay updated on the most important news stories for Scorpio Tankers by adding it to your watchlist or portfolio. Alternatively, explore our Community to discover new perspectives on Scorpio Tankers.

📰 Beyond the headline: 4 risks and 2 things going right for Scorpio Tankers that every investor should see.

This fleet reshuffle is a sizeable move for Scorpio Tankers because it swaps nine 2014 built product tankers for a pipeline of newer MR and LR2 ships plus two very large crude carriers (VLCCs). On the disposal side, roughly US$300 million of agreed vessel sales and the repayment of around US$32 million of related revolving credit gives the company extra financial flexibility and slightly lowers balance sheet risk. On the growth side, committing to ten newbuildings, including crude exposure through VLCCs, gradually tilts the asset base and earnings mix away from a pure product tanker story.

For you as an investor, the trade off is clear. In the near term, the company will have fewer on the water vessels, which can cap spot exposure and rate upside during the transition. Over 2026 to 2029, the delivery schedule introduces new capacity that could support earnings if product and crude markets stay supportive. It also raises capital spending and execution risk. Against a context of flat long term revenue, a shrinking fleet and earnings per share pressure, this repositioning reads as an attempt to refresh the portfolio rather than simply grow for growth’s sake.

Advertisement

How This Fits Into The Scorpio Tankers Narrative

  • The emphasis on younger, more efficient tonnage aligns with the narrative that a modern fleet can capture trade flow shifts and support margins as refining patterns change.
  • Adding VLCCs and increasing order book exposure introduces more sensitivity to future capacity cycles, which the narrative already flags as a threat to long term profitability.
  • The specific timing and scale of these newbuilding commitments, including deliveries out to 2029, are not fully reflected in the existing narrative’s discussion of future capex needs.

Knowing what a company is worth starts with understanding its story. Check out one of the top narratives in the Simply Wall St Community for Scorpio Tankers to help decide what it's worth to you.

The Risks and Rewards Investors Should Consider

  • ⚠️ Analysts highlight that earnings are forecast to decline by an average of 3.3% per year over the next 3 years, so higher capital commitments add pressure on future returns.
  • ⚠️ Rising fleet age related costs and the broader risk of tanker overcapacity could weigh on utilization and margins once this larger order book delivers into the market.
  • 🎁 Trading at what is assessed as 31.4% below an estimate of fair value gives some room for the fleet reshuffle to add value if execution and market conditions line up.
  • 🎁 The company is described as having high quality earnings and trading at good value compared with peers, which may support investor confidence while the fleet mix is reset.

What To Watch Going Forward

From here, keep an eye on three things. First, the actual sale prices, closing timeline and any extra debt reduction tied to the nine disposals. Second, the progress and cost discipline on MR, LR2 and VLCC newbuild deliveries through 2026 to 2029, especially if product tanker peers such as Torm and Hafnia or crude focused names like Frontline pursue similar ordering. Third, how this leaner but younger fleet affects Scorpio Tankers’ revenue, margins and cash flow in future results, given the recent pattern of flat long term revenue and softer earnings per share. Any change in analyst risk flags, such as the current set of 2 rewards and 4 risks, will also be useful context as the repositioning plays out.

Sometimes these stories can cloud the minds of investors, leading them to invest with their emotions rather than on the merit of good company fundamentals. To the delight of shareholders, Marine Electricals (India) has achieved impressive annual EPS growth of 40%, compound, over the last three years. That sort of growth rarely ever lasts long, but it is well worth paying attention to when it happens.

Top-line growth is a great indicator that growth is sustainable, and combined with a high earnings before interest and taxation (EBIT) margin, it's a great way for a company to maintain a competitive advantage in the market. EBIT margins for Marine Electricals (India) remained fairly unchanged over the last year, however the company should be pleased to report its revenue growth for the period of 13% to ₹8.5b. For beginners, it can seem like a good idea (and an exciting prospect) to buy a company that tells a good story to investors, even if it currently lacks a track record of revenue and profit. But as Peter Lynch said in One Up On Wall Street, 'Long shots almost never pay off.' While a well funded company may sustain losses for years, it will need to generate a profit eventually, or else investors will move on and the company will wither away.

In contrast to all that, many investors prefer to focus on companies like Jayaswal Neco Industries (NSE:JAYNECOIND), which has not only revenues, but also profits. Even if this company is fairly valued by the market, investors would agree that generating consistent profits will continue to provide Jayaswal Neco Industries with the means to add long-term value to shareholders.

AI is about to change healthcare. These 20 stocks are working on everything from early diagnostics to drug discovery. The best part - they are all under $10bn in marketcap - there is still time to get in early.

Advertisement

How Quickly Is Jayaswal Neco Industries Increasing Earnings Per Share?

If you believe that markets are even vaguely efficient, then over the long term you'd expect a company's share price to follow its earnings per share (EPS) outcomes. That makes EPS growth an attractive quality for any company. Impressively, Jayaswal Neco Industries has grown EPS by 27% per year, compound, in the last three years. If growth like this continues on into the future, then shareholders will have plenty to smile about.

It's often helpful to take a look at earnings before interest and tax (EBIT) margins, as well as revenue growth, to get another take on the quality of the company's growth. The music to the ears of Jayaswal Neco Industries shareholders is that EBIT margins have grown from 11% to 15% in the last 12 months and revenues are on an upwards trend as well. That's great to see, on both counts.

You can take a look at the company's revenue and earnings growth trend, in the chart below. To see the actual numbers, click on the chart.

View our latest analysis for Jayaswal Neco Industries

While it's always good to see growing profits, you should always remember that a weak balance sheet could come back to bite. So check Jayaswal Neco Industries' balance sheet strength, before getting too excited.

Are Jayaswal Neco Industries Insiders Aligned With All Shareholders?

It should give investors a sense of security owning shares in a company if insiders also own shares, creating a close alignment their interests. Shareholders will be pleased by the fact that insiders own Jayaswal Neco Industries shares worth a considerable sum. With a whopping ₹5.7b worth of shares as a group, insiders have plenty riding on the company's success. That's certainly enough to let shareholders know that management will be very focussed on long term growth.

It means a lot to see insiders invested in the business, but shareholders may be wondering if remuneration policies are in their best interest. Well, based on the CEO pay, you'd argue that they are indeed. Our analysis has discovered that the median total compensation for the CEOs of companies like Jayaswal Neco Industries with market caps between ₹38b and ₹151b is about ₹37m.

Jayaswal Neco Industries' CEO took home a total compensation package worth ₹28m in the year leading up to March 2025. That is actually below the median for CEO's of similarly sized companies. CEO compensation is hardly the most important aspect of a company to consider, but when it's reasonable, that gives a little more confidence that leadership are looking out for shareholder interests. Generally, arguments can be made that reasonable pay levels attest to good decision-making.

Is Jayaswal Neco Industries Worth Keeping An Eye On?

For growth investors, Jayaswal Neco Industries' raw rate of earnings growth is a beacon in the night. If that's not enough, consider also that the CEO pay is quite reasonable, and insiders are well-invested alongside other shareholders. The overarching message here is that Jayaswal Neco Industries has underlying strengths that make it worth a look at. What about risks? Every company has them, and we've spotted 1 warning sign for Jayaswal Neco Industries you should know about.

There's always the possibility of doing well buying stocks that are not growing earnings and do not have insiders buying shares. But for those who consider these important metrics, we encourage you to check out companies that do have those features. You can access a tailored list of Indian companies which have demonstrated growth backed by significant insider holdings.

Please note the insider transactions discussed in this article refer to reportable transactions in the relevant jurisdiction.

data.title: Microsoft OpenAI Reset Tests Azure AI Edge And Valuation Story
data.description:
• Microsoft and OpenAI have revised their partnership, ending exclusivity on licensing and revenue sharing.
  • The change allows OpenAI to work with cloud competitors such as Google and Amazon.
  • Microsoft keeps a long term license to OpenAI technology while no longer paying a revenue share.
  • The shift affects Azure's AI positioning and could influence how enterprises choose AI cloud providers.

For investors watching NasdaqGS:MSFT, the move comes with the stock trading at $424.82 after a 42.3% return over 3 years and 75.8% over 5 years. Over the past month, the shares show a 19.1% return, with a 9.4% return over 1 year and 1.6% over the last 7 days, while year to date performance reflects a 10.2% decline.

This reset of the Microsoft OpenAI relationship removes a unique Azure selling point but keeps core access to OpenAI models that underlie products such as Copilot. Investors may want to watch how Microsoft positions its own in house AI stack, how aggressively rivals court OpenAI workloads, and whether reduced regulatory pressure offsets the loss of exclusivity over time.

data.title: Is Deere (DE) Attractively Priced After Recent 21.6% Year To Date Gain?
data.description:
• If you are wondering whether Deere's current share price reflects its underlying worth, the key question is how that price stacks up against a range of valuation tests.
  • The stock recently closed at US$567.69, with returns of 21.6% year to date and 25.0% over the last year, alongside a 4.5% decline over the last 7 days and a 0.2% move over the last 30 days that may signal changing sentiment.
  • Recent coverage has focused on Deere as a major player in capital goods, with attention on how its share price performance compares to peers and broad sector trends. This context has kept the stock in focus for investors who are weighing whether the current level offers attractive value or embeds higher perceived risk.
  • On Simply Wall St's framework, Deere currently has a valuation score of 2 out of 6. The next step is to unpack what that means across different valuation approaches and then round out the picture with a broader way of thinking about value at the end of this article.

Deere scores just 2/6 on our valuation checks. See what other red flags we found in the full valuation breakdown.

Advertisement

Approach 1: Deere Discounted Cash Flow (DCF) Analysis

The Discounted Cash Flow model estimates what a business could be worth by projecting its future cash flows and then discounting those back to today's value using a required return.

For Deere, the model used is a 2 Stage Free Cash Flow to Equity approach. The latest twelve month free cash flow is about $3.7b. Analysts provide explicit forecasts for the next few years, and Simply Wall St then extrapolates those to build a ten year path, with projected free cash flow of $12.4b in 2030. All of these future cash flows are expressed in dollars and then discounted to reflect time and risk.

On this basis, the DCF model arrives at an estimated intrinsic value of about $673.38 per share. Compared with the recent share price of $567.69, this implies Deere trades at roughly a 15.7% discount to that intrinsic value, so on this measure the shares screen as undervalued.

Result: UNDERVALUED

Our Discounted Cash Flow (DCF) analysis suggests Deere is undervalued by 15.7%. Track this in your watchlist or portfolio, or discover 53 more high quality undervalued stocks.

Head to the Valuation section of our Company Report for more details on how we arrive at this Fair Value for Deere.

Approach 2: Deere Price vs Earnings

For a profitable company like Deere, the P/E ratio is a commonly used yardstick because it links what you pay per share to the earnings that each share generates. Investors usually accept a higher P/E when they expect stronger growth or see lower risk, and a lower P/E when they are pricing in weaker growth or higher risk.

Deere currently trades on a P/E of 31.85x. That sits above the Machinery industry average P/E of 27.73x and the peer group average of 24.86x. On simple comparisons, the stock is priced at a higher multiple of earnings than many industry peers.

Simply Wall St also provides a Fair Ratio of 39.86x for Deere. This is a proprietary estimate of what a reasonable P/E could be, given factors such as the company’s earnings profile, industry, profit margins, market capitalization and risk characteristics. Because it blends these elements rather than relying only on peer or sector averages, it can offer a more tailored reference point for the stock.

When comparing the current P/E of 31.85x with the Fair Ratio of 39.86x, the shares trade below that modelled level. On this metric, the stock appears to be priced below that reference point.

Result: UNDERVALUED

Earlier sections covered DCF and P/E, but there is an easier way to connect those numbers to your own view on Deere, and that is through Narratives. Narratives let you write a clear story about the company, link that story to specific forecasts for future revenue, earnings and margins, and see the Fair Value that falls out of those assumptions, all within Simply Wall St's Community page that is used by millions of investors.

Each Narrative ties a qualitative view to a quantified forecast and a resulting Fair Value. It then sets that Fair Value right beside the current share price so you can decide whether the price looks high or low relative to your story and consider what that might mean for your own timing.

Narratives update automatically when fresh information such as earnings, guidance or news is added to the platform. This helps keep your Fair Value aligned with the latest data without requiring you to rebuild your model from scratch.

For Deere, one investor might build a bullish Narrative that looks like the US$793 fair value case, with assumptions about revenue growth, margins above 19% and earnings around US$10.3b. Another might align with the US$500 fair value case, using flatter revenue, margins nearer 13% and earnings around US$6.1b. By comparing those different Fair Values to the live price, you can quickly see which story matches your own expectations.
